retrieved_docs.title: Prevention, Evaluation, and Management of Coronavirus (COVID-19) in Pregnancy and Puerperium -- Treatment / Management -- Medical Management
retrieved_docs.content: Monoclonal antibody therapy: It is presently recommended for patients with mild COVID-19 and risk factors for progression to severe disease. Currently, monoclonal antibodies with EUA are bamlanivimab-etesivimab, casirivimab-imdevimab, and sotrovimab. [39] The NIH guidelines note pregnancy as an independent risk factor for severe disease. Though there is no large, direct pregnancy-related data, the risk-benefit ratio and the previous evidence for tolerance of other IgG antibodies suggest that one must consider their use even in pregnancy. [40] The American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG) notes that, at times, a patient's pregnancy may be the only risk factor, and, in such cases, patients with an additional risk factor may be considered for monoclonal antibody therapy. An additional indication for monoclonal antibody therapy is for post-exposure prophylaxis in unvaccinated, incompletely vaccinated, and those with factors that would lead to poor immune response development. [41]

retrieved_docs.title: Teprotumumab -- Contraindications -- Warnings and Precautions
retrieved_docs.content: Teprotumumab has no listed contraindications on the label. However, warnings and precautions are for poorly controlled diabetics, pregnant or breastfeeding women, and prepubertal children. Based on its mechanism of action, it is likely that teprotumumab will have teratogenic effects during pregnancy. Therefore, it is recommended that women of child-bearing age use adequate contraception before starting therapy, during the course of treatment, and for six months following the last dose of teprotumumab.

retrieved_docs.title: Etesevimab -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnant patients: Etesevimab and bamlanivimab can only be prescribed for pregnant patients with SARS-CoV-2 only if the benefit overbalances the risk for the duo (fetus and mother). Being an IgG antibody, both bamlanivimab and etesevimab can cross the placenta and reach the fetus. However, whether this placental transfer is beneficial or risky for the growing fetus is unclear.

retrieved_docs.title: Bamlanivimab -- Toxicity -- Specific Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy: Human IgG1 antibodies can cross the placental barrier, so the drug has the potential to do the same. The FDA recommends using the drug in pregnant women if the potential benefit outweighs the risk to both the mother and the fetus. There is no sufficient data regarding birth defects secondary to drug use.

retrieved_docs.title: Trastuzumab -- Contraindications -- Warnings and Precautions
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy: Trastuzumab is thought to be a human teratogen. Fetal exposure is associated with oligohydramnios, leading to pulmonary hypoplasia and death. Patients who may become pregnant should use at least one form of contraception during trastuzumab therapy. Whether trastuzumab is secreted in human breast milk or not is unclear.

retrieved_docs.title: Nivolumab -- Contraindications -- Warnings and Precautions
retrieved_docs.content: Contraindications to nivolumab include sensitivity to the drug or its ingredients and multiple myeloma. However, some important settings have not been addressed in clinical studies, such as drug interaction, use in pregnancy, and use during breastfeeding. [28] Some studies on animal models have shown embryofetal toxicity. As human IgG4 is known to cross the placental barrier, there is a potential pathway for transmitting antibodies to a developing fetus. [29] The use of nivolumab during pregnancy should be carefully considered in cases in which the potential benefits outweigh the risks to the patient and fetus. [30] There are literature reports of successful, viable pregnancies in patients who received nivolumab as monotherapy or combined with ipilimumab. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Rubella -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Deterrence and patient education regarding rubella focus on preventing transmission, ensuring immunity, and minimizing the risk of CRS. Healthcare practitioners must educate patients, especially women of childbearing age, about the importance of rubella immunity and the need for vaccination before conception. Routine screening for rubella-specific IgG antibodies during early pregnancy helps identify susceptible individuals, enabling timely counseling and postpartum immunization if necessary.

retrieved_docs.title: MMR Vaccine -- Contraindications -- Contraindications of Vaccines
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy or planning should be discouraged within 28 days of vaccination due to the risk of congenital rubella. However, performing a pre-vaccination pregnancy test is unnecessary if the patient denies pregnancy.

retrieved_docs.title: Digoxin Immune Fab -- Contraindications -- Warning and Precautions
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy: The FDA has designated Fab products former pregnancy category C. This rating indicates that they should be used cautiously and only if the benefit justifies the risks imposed on the fetus. Whether or not Fab products can harm the fetus or affect future reproductive ability is unknown. Due to a lack of human and animal studies, no clear safety data exist. Therefore, digoxin immune Fab should only be administered during pregnancy if needed.

retrieved_docs.title: Antiphospholipid Syndrome  -- Treatment / Management -- Pregnancy Management
retrieved_docs.content: In women with definite obstetric APS with recurrent pregnancy complications despite combination treatment with low-dose aspirin and prophylactic heparin/LMWH, increasing the heparin dose to a therapeutic dose or adding hydroxychloroquine or low-dose prednisolone in the first trimester may be considered. Using intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) might be considered in highly selected cases. Although the expected benefit of IVIG is small, the panel agreed that this could be considered in highly selected cases when other treatments have failed.

retrieved_docs.title: Prevention, Evaluation, and Management of Coronavirus (COVID-19) in Pregnancy and Puerperium -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: A patient's travel history has become more relevant in the present pandemic. [18] Policy decisions regarding diagnostic algorithms are likely to be influenced by the location. Contact history is also extremely important. Decisions regarding quarantine, assessment of the risk of progression to severe disease, and exploring post-exposure prophylaxis depend on a good contact history. While examining an obstetric patient, one should now ask for a vaccination history and a history of being previously infected with COVID-19. Patients who are incompletely vaccinated or unvaccinated are at higher risk of progressing to severe COVID-19. [19] Similarly, with waning immunity, it is also relevant to know the date of the final dose in a vaccination series. Knowing the patient's COVID-19 illness history is vital for deciding when to vaccinate next. [20]  The relevant treatment history if previously infected would include knowing whether a monoclonal antibody product was used, which may further influence the timing of vaccination.

retrieved_docs.title: Threatened Miscarriage -- Treatment / Management -- Prevent Alloimmunization
retrieved_docs.content: If given in the first trimester, a dose of Rh(D)-immune globulin 50-120 mcg IM once is effective at alloimmunization prevention; after 12 weeks of gestation, Rh(D)-immune globulin 300 mcg IM once is typically preferred in the US, though other guidelines recommend lower doses depending on the situation. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Golimumab -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy considerations: Golimumab use during pregnancy may increase the risk of infections in newborns for up to 6 months after birth. Infants exposed to golimumab in utero should not receive any immunization with live vaccines for 6 months following the mother's last golimumab dose. The American College of Rheumatology (ACR) discusses the impact of placental transfer and fetal exposure for most biologic therapies, which varies with gestational stage. Biologics containing an IgG1 Fc region typically do not enter the fetal circulation until the second trimester. During the third trimester, administration of TNF inhibitors with these structural components (eg, infliximab, etanercept, adalimumab, and golimumab) results in placental transfer and notable neonatal drug levels. Limited evidence suggests no adverse effects, especially in the first trimester. The ACR guidelines recommend discontinuing these medications for the third trimester if the disease is well-controlled. However, if the disease is active, continuing TNF inhibitors to delivery is advised, though the guidelines acknowledge the likelihood of significant fetal drug exposure. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Brodalumab -- Contraindications -- Children and Pregnancy
retrieved_docs.content: Though no contraindication exists, researchers have not investigated the efficacy and safety of brodalumab as a potential treatment in children or pregnant women with psoriasis or breastfed infants of mothers taking brodalumab. However, monoclonal antibodies are generally known to actively be transported through the placenta, especially in the third trimester, and get excreted in modest amounts in breast milk. Risks and benefits to the mother require assessment before initiation or discontinuation of brodalumab.

retrieved_docs.title: Nivolumab -- Contraindications -- Warnings and Precautions
retrieved_docs.content: Contraception for at least 5 months after the last dose of nivolumab is strongly advised. Breastfeeding concomitantly to receiving nivolumab therapy is discouraged as antibodies might be passed onto the breast milk. Studies evaluating the fertility of patients receiving nivolumab have not yet been published; it remains unknown whether nivolumab affects either female or male fertility. Patients should be thoroughly informed on this matter, and reproductive counseling should be offered to patients in reproductive age groups. Although preexisting conditions of an autoimmune or inflammatory nature are not a contraindication for nivolumab therapy, it has been reported that such patients have increased rates of immunosuppressive therapy and hospitalization after ICI treatment. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Vertical Transplacental Infections -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Deterrence of transplacental infections starts with comprehensive patient education on avoiding exposures that could lead to such infections. (Please refer to the Etiology section for more information on infection prevention.) Clinicians should emphasize the significance of regular prenatal care and follow-up, which allows for early detection and management of potential infections. Vaccination advice for preventable diseases, such as rubella and varicella, is a critical component of preconceptual counseling and care. Through proactive patient education and preventive measures, clinicians can significantly reduce the risk of transplacental infections, protecting maternal and fetal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 567
retrieved_docs.content: (cerclage, drug contraindications), and risk profiles (high‑risk status, poor reproductive history) converge to inform comprehensive assessment and management of female reproductive and respiratory health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 189
retrieved_docs.content: each condition as a potential adverse event, enabling systematic risk assessment, targeted monitoring, operative decision‑making, and coordinated maternal‑fetal and newborn care aimed at reducing morbidity and improving outcomes for both mother and infant.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 209
retrieved_docs.content: constitute major complications of pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium. Recognizing the placement of each specific condition within its broader category supports comprehensive assessment, risk stratification, and coordinated management of pregnant patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: assessment, early identification of abnormal presentations, cervical pathology, uterine functional disturbances, and multiple‑gestation status, together with avoidance of teratogenic drugs, are essential for individualized management strategies that optimize maternal and neonatal outcomes during labor and delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 109
retrieved_docs.content: disorders, and obstetric interventions together form a comprehensive framework for risk assessment, care planning, and management of maternal‑fetal outcomes during pregnancy, delivery, and the postpartum period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1021
retrieved_docs.content: organizes general obstetric procedure categories (إجراء توليدي, Antepartum Procedures, Obstetrics incision) and specific clinical actions—ranging from diagnostic sampling and membrane management to fetal stimulation, reduction of multiple gestations, and surgical treatment of ectopic pregnancies—providing a comprehensive framework for coordinating obstetric care across the ante‑partum, intrapartum, and postpartum periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2316
retrieved_docs.content: A poor reproductive history, reflecting previous obstetric complications or adverse outcomes, is clinically associated with an increased risk of preterm labor (مخاض قبل الأوان). This connection highlights the importance of detailed reproductive assessments in prenatal care to identify women who may be more likely to experience early delivery and to apply targeted monitoring and preventive strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 777
retrieved_docs.content: and uterine scars from previous surgeries, including previous cesarean sections, are specific indications that belong to the larger group of maternal‑care indications triggered by known or suspected pelvic‑organ abnormalities. These indications guide clinical decision‑making throughout pregnancy, labor, and delivery, and they are consistently represented across Dutch and Arabic terminology, ensuring unified understanding and management of maternal health risks.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2304
retrieved_docs.content: Early, first or unspecified trimester hemorrhage is classified as spotting complicating pregnancy, unspecified trimester.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1607
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy is a broad category that includes several specific maternal health issues. It has the child condition قَيْء الحمل خفيف الوطأة (light vomiting of pregnancy) and is classified as encompassing الاعتماد على المخدرات، قبل الولادة (drug dependence before delivery), الحصبة الألمانية خلال فترة الحمل (German measles during pregnancy), and خلل وظيفة الدرقية في فترة الحمل (thyroid dysfunction in the gestational period). These various complications highlight the diverse risks that can affect pregnant individuals and underscore the importance of comprehensive prenatal assessment and appropriate management to protect both maternal and fetal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 157
retrieved_docs.content: these entities form an interconnected network that integrates obstetric, perinatal, neonatal screening, and renal‑surgical conditions, supporting comprehensive risk stratification, coordinated maternal‑fetal care, and ongoing management of delayed postpartum effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2849
retrieved_docs.content: Poor reproductive history and elective termination of pregnancy are clinically associated with preterm labor (مخاض قبل الأوان). This relationship indicates that women who have experienced a suboptimal reproductive history or who have undergone an elective termination of pregnancy may be at increased risk for developing preterm labor. Recognizing these associations can help clinicians identify patients who require closer monitoring and targeted interventions to prevent or manage preterm labor, thereby improving maternal and neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1697
retrieved_docs.content: A caesarean section scar of unknown type and a poor reproductive history are clinically associated with مخاض قبل الأوان (preterm labor), indicating that women who have an indeterminate type of previous cesarean scar or who have experienced suboptimal reproductive outcomes are at increased risk for premature labor. Recognizing these associations enables clinicians to identify high‑risk patients, implement closer surveillance, and consider preventive strategies to reduce the likelihood of preterm delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 752
retrieved_docs.content: essential for clinicians to anticipate potential delivery complications, decide when normal delivery procedures are feasible, and implement appropriate interventions to optimize maternal and neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY is classified as a subcategory within the broader group of Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium, indicating that these health issues are primarily associated with the gestational period and its immediate aftermath.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1600
retrieved_docs.content: Triamcinolone is a corticosteroid that may be employed in the treatment of multiple sclerosis (التصلب اللويحي). However, its use is contraindicated in the setting of pregnancy (حالَةٌ رَبْوِيَّة). Clinicians should therefore balance the potential benefit of triamcinolone for managing multiple sclerosis against the risk of adverse effects in pregnant patients and consider alternative therapies when a pregnancy is present.
